I want to go through the actual patterns themselves for the opening, for the body, and for the clothes. And this will allow you to write your scripts very, very effectively in a fraction of the time. Now, when I go into a corporation and train the sales forces, I'll actually tell each salesperson to create five or six of their own language patterns for each of the three tens. I'm talking about chunks they can use that are intelligent, ethical, persuasive, and of course sexy. They'll write five about themselves, meaning to sell themselves. They'll write five about selling the company that they work for. Then they'll have five specific language patterns for selling the product. And then what we do is we actually pool all of those together and pick out the best of the best, and we create one master script. Okay? That's a very good exercise. So a couple of salespeople you're friends with, you go through this together. We each create language patterns for each of the three tens, and then you pool them together, pick out the best of the best, and develop one master script. So let's walk through some of the key language patterns here for the three elements of the script, the open, the body, and the clothes. And of course we'll start with the opening. Okay? Now, number one, when you introduce yourself, it's something along the lines of this. You know, hey, John, it's Jordan calling from Sales Security in Melbourne. How's it going today? Right? You sound enthusiastic. You're good. You get people keyed into your voice. Okay? So it's not like, you know, hi, it's John there. Hi, John. Jordan. It's like, hey, it's John there. Hey, John, Jordan, Belfort calling from Sales Security. How you doing today? You know, upbeat, enthusiastic. Okay? Number two, next pattern is going to be to their response to them saying, I'm okay. You say, great. Now, John, if you recall, we met last Thursday night over at the Marriott Hotel. Now, again, I'm just making up, just, you know, making up words here. So this just has to relate to what your actual business is, how you contact your client. But again, this is just an example. So you would say, you know, now, John, if you recall, we met last Thursday night over at the Marriott Hotel with one of your top salespeople, James Arnell. Does that ring a bell? Essentially, you're reinserting yourself into the moment you met the guy. And again, you're looking for it to say, yeah, I guess that rings a bell, right? And when you say, does that ring a bell? You're not saying, does that ring a bell? Huh-huh. You're, does that ring a bell? Like, you know, reasonable, no big deal. Okay. Now, number three, the next pattern here is something that's extremely important. And that is basically the reason for your call. You're justifying why you're calling the person. Now, listen to my tone very closely. I say, you know, how's it going? They say, great. I say, great. Well, the reason for the call, okay, has been reaching out to people in your area, or something just came across my desk, or last time we spoke, you told me that, is a reason why you're calling them. Okay. You know, you're contacting people on our list right now. You know, reason for the call today is reaching out to people in your area. Not like, John, the reason for the call today is you get it? That doesn't do the trick. Okay. So remember, there's got to be a reason why you're calling the person. I didn't just call you out of the blue. I didn't call you yesterday. I didn't call you tomorrow. I called you right now. And there's got to be a reason why attached that. And again, I've been through this before. It's called a justifier. And it gives you that valid reason why you're calling today. Okay. And it dramatically will increase your compliance rate. So I might say, well, John, the reason for the call today is it's been reaching out to people in your area. And notice how I dropped my tone a bit. Okay. Now, number four, from here, I go directly into the qualifying portion of my script. And I start by asking for permission to ask questions. And again, this is another example of using a justifier. And this time it's with the word so. For example, I'd say, John, just a couple of quick questions. So I don't waste your time. So the word so is my justifier. And it essentially gives me a reason why I need to ask him questions. And that reason is so I don't waste his time. Okay. And it's very, very important. You always want to ask for permission to qualify. Okay. And now, number five, the last part of your open is always a transition. Okay. And hopefully you know this by now, but I'll say it anyway. Well, John, based on everything you just said to me, this is definitely a perfect fit for you. And that statement should become an anchor for you. You should know it by heart. Make sense? Okay. Great. Thanks. Bye. Bye. Bye. Bye.
